title: Plastic waste generation
subtitle: This measures total plastic waste generation prior to management and therefore does not represent the quantity of plastic at risk of polluting waterways, rivers and the ocean environment.
caption:
Plastic waste generation, 2010

This measures total plastic waste generation prior to management and therefore does not represent the quantity of plastic at risk of polluting waterways, rivers and the ocean environment.

The choropleth map displays global plastic waste generation in 2010, using a continuous color scale ranging from light beige for zero tonnes to deep maroon for 10 million tonnes, with hatched patterns indicating countries where no data is available. The United States and China stand out as the largest generators, both shaded in the darkest maroon band representing 10 million tonnes. Brazil also falls within this highest category, sharing the deep red hue with the two northern hemisphere giants. Moving down the scale, Russia, India, and Germany appear in the 7.5 to 10 million tonne range, shown in a slightly lighter but still intense red. Mid-range values between 2.5 and 5 million tonnes cover nations such as Mexico, Indonesia, and South Africa, visible in medium orange tones. Lower generation levels, between 0 and 1 million tonnes, dominate much of sub-Saharan Africa, Central Asia, and parts of South America including Bolivia and Paraguay, all rendered in pale beige. Countries like Canada, Australia, and most of Western Europe sit in the 1 to 2.5 million tonne band, colored in light orange. The hatched pattern appears over Greenland, Western Sahara, and several small island states, signaling absence of reported figures. The distribution shows concentration in large-population, industrialized economies, while vast geographic areas — particularly across Africa and Central Asia — remain in the lowest bands or lack data entirely. The visual structure emphasizes disparity: a few countries generate orders of magnitude more plastic waste than the majority of nations on the map.
